div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
Ariel generates step-by-step diagram
walkthroughs to help humans understand
complex systems. It is most useful in
READMEs and pull request summaries

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
LLMs generate code much faster than
engineers. Once upon a time, our
bottleneck was how fast we could write
code. Now, the bottleneck is reviewing
AI-generated code

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
While the code AIs generate is usually
valid on a low-level, is still useful
(perhaps critical) for human engineers
to comprehend and evaluate the
architecture.

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
Automatically generating diagrams helps.
Now, instead of building a mental model
from scratch, the reviewer has a map to
cross-reference against the code.

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
However, diagrams without narrative
still leave reviewers context-switching
— toggling between diagram and code,
with no prescribed reading order.

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
Working memory has a hard ceiling.
Overloaded reviewers rubber-stamp.
Comprehending reviewers make better
decisions.

div.style: color: rgb(232, 234, 240); display: inline-block; white-space: nowrap;
div.span:
ariel sequences the narrative — one
idea per step, one visual change per
idea. Working memory stays within its
limits. Understanding builds
incrementally.
